hook: You already know that triangles have angle bisectors and perpendicular bisectors, and that those special lines meet at points with useful properties. Now imagine you hang a cardboard triangle from a string — where exactly do you attach the string so the triangle balances perfectly flat? That balance point is called the centroid, and finding it requires understanding medians. The question is: if you know the vertices of a triangle, how do you locate that perfect balance point, and why does it always sit exactly two-thirds of the way along each median?
concept: A median of a triangle is a segment drawn from any vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. Every triangle has exactly three medians, and — remarkably — all three medians always intersect at a single point called the centroid. The centroid divides each median in the ratio $2:1$, meaning it is located $\frac{2}{3}$ of the way from each vertex to the opposite midpoint. An altitude, by contrast, is a perpendicular segment from a vertex to the line containing the opposite side; altitudes meet at the orthocenter.
anatomy: In this topic there are four key geometric elements to name precisely. The median connects a vertex (such as vertex $A$) to the midpoint $M$ of the opposite side (such as $\overline{BC}$), so the midpoint is always the endpoint of the median, not an arbitrary point. The centroid, labeled $G$, is the point where all three medians concurrently intersect. The Centroid Ratio gives the exact split: $AG = \frac{2}{3}AM$ (the vertex-to-centroid piece) and $GM = \frac{1}{3}AM$ (the centroid-to-midpoint piece), where $AM$ is the full median length. The altitude is a separate segment that is perpendicular to the opposite side, and its concurrent point is the orthocenter, not the centroid.

worked_example.description_latex: We are given that $AM$ is a full median of length 18, meaning $M$ is the midpoint of the side opposite vertex $A$. Before applying any formula, we identify that $G$ is the centroid sitting on this median, which guarantees the $2:1$ split we will use in the next step.

The Centroid Ratio theorem states that the centroid divides every median so that the vertex-to-centroid distance equals $\frac{2}{3}$ of the full median length. We write this formula now because $AG$ is the piece from the vertex to the centroid, which is the larger of the two segments.
Substituting $AM = 18$ gives $AG = 12$. This is the distance from vertex $A$ all the way to the centroid $G$, which represents two-thirds of the journey along the median.

The remaining segment from the centroid to the midpoint is simply the full median minus the piece we just found: $18 - 12 = 6$. Notice this equals $\frac{1}{3} \cdot 18$, confirming the $2:1$ ratio: $AG : GM = 12 : 6 = 2 : 1$.

partial_example.description_latex: Here we are given the vertex-to-centroid piece $BG = 14$ and asked to find the full median length $BN$. We know $G$ is the centroid, so the $2:1$ Centroid Ratio still applies — but this time we need to work backward from the shorter given piece to the whole.

We write the Centroid Ratio formula with $BG$ as the vertex-to-centroid distance, which equals $\frac{2}{3}$ of the full median $BN$. Now we have an equation with one unknown, $BN$, that we can solve by isolating it.
Solve for $BN$ by substituting $BG = 14$ into the equation $BG = \frac{2}{3} \cdot BN$. What is the full length of the median $BN$?
Once we have $BN = 21$, the centroid-to-midpoint piece $GN$ is simply one-third of $21$, giving $GN = 7$. You can double-check: $BG + GN = 14 + 7 = 21 = BN$, so the segments add up correctly.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Median } \overline{CP} \text{ has length } 30. \text{ Find } CG \text{ and } GP, \text{ where } G \text{ is the centroid.}
\text{The centroid } G \text{ divides median } \overline{AK} \text{ so that } AG = 10. \text{ Find the full length } AK.
\text{In } \triangle XYZ, \text{ the centroid } G \text{ lies on median } \overline{YM}. \text{ If } GM = 8, \text{ find } YG \text{ and } YM.
\text{The centroid of } \triangle ABC \text{ is at } G. \text{ Median } \overline{BD} \text{ has } BD = 3x + 6 \text{ and } BG = 2x + 2. \text{ Find } x \text{ and } BD.

\text{A triangular metal plate is balanced on a pin at its centroid } G. \text{ One median measures } 27 \text{ cm. How far is the centroid from the vertex end, and how far from the midpoint end, of that median?}

common_mistakes: Students confuse which piece of the median equals two-thirds: they write $GM = \frac{2}{3} \cdot AM$ (the midpoint piece) instead of $AG = \frac{2}{3} \cdot AM$ (the vertex piece). Remember that the centroid is closer to the midpoint, so the vertex-to-centroid distance is always the larger piece, equal to $\frac{2}{3}$ of the whole.

When given the vertex-to-centroid length and asked for the full median, students multiply by $\frac{2}{3}$ instead of dividing. If $AG = \frac{2}{3} \cdot AM$, then $AM = \frac{3}{2} \cdot AG$; always set up the equation first and solve algebraically rather than guessing which operation to use.

Students mix up the centroid (where medians meet) and the orthocenter (where altitudes meet), applying the $2:1$ ratio to altitude lengths. The $2:1$ Centroid Ratio applies only to medians, not to altitudes; if the problem mentions perpendicular segments from vertices, you are dealing with altitudes and the centroid ratio does not apply.
untested_variants: Finding the centroid coordinates using the midpoint and coordinate geometry formula $G = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2+x_3}{3},\, \frac{y_1+y_2+y_3}{3}\right)$ when given vertex coordinates.
Problems requiring students to first find the midpoint of a side using the Midpoint Formula and then compute median or centroid lengths, combining two separate skills.
Proofs or justification problems asking students to verify that all three medians of a given triangle are concurrent at a point satisfying the $2:1$ ratio.
Problems involving the orthocenter, including identifying whether the orthocenter lies inside, on, or outside a triangle based on triangle type (acute, right, obtuse).
